title: Coloured marzipan fruits
subtitle: Hand-shaped marzipan in miniature fruit forms with food colouring
excerpt: Marzipan fruits are moulded by hand from a raw marzipan made with ground almonds, icing sugar, and egg white. Each piece is shaped into a miniature fruit, painted with food colouring, and left to dry. A traditional confectionery craft that requires only patience and a light touch.
sourceNotes: Marzipan modelling is documented in European confectionery literature from the medieval period; coloured marzipan fruits appear in public-domain British and continental confectionery manuals from the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The technique here follows the standard uncooked marzipan method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marzipan fruits dry and harden over 24 to 48 hours. Once dry, keep in a single layer in an airtight tin for up to 4 weeks.

glossaryTerms.definition: Raw (uncooked) marzipan is made by combining ground almonds with icing sugar and a small amount of egg white to bind. It has a pliable, dough-like consistency that can be shaped, rolled, and moulded without cooking. It differs from commercial ready-to-roll marzipan (which is cooked) in flavour and texture: raw marzipan is slightly coarser and more intensely almond-flavoured. Food colouring is applied after shaping using a fine brush or by kneading a tiny amount directly into the marzipan mass.

content.text: Making marzipan fruits is a slow craft, not a fast recipe. Each fruit is shaped individually; the modelling takes much longer than the mixing.

is soft and easy to model when freshly made; it stiffens as it dries. Work in a cool room with cool hands. The colouring is the most satisfying part: a small paintbrush and gel food colours applied to dry or nearly dry shapes give the best results. Simple shapes (oranges, lemons, strawberries) are the easiest place to start.

items.prepNote: gel food colours in red, yellow, green, orange; a very small amount of each

content.text: Combine the ground almonds and icing sugar in a bowl. Add the almond extract if using. Add the egg white a little at a time, mixing until the mixture comes together into a smooth, pliable dough. You may not need all the egg white; stop as soon as it coheres without being sticky. The marzipan can be used immediately.

Divide the marzipan into portions and roll each into a ball about the size of a large grape. Shape each ball into the form of a fruit: roll an orange shape by rolling smoothly between your palms and dimpling the ends with a finger; create a strawberry shape by pressing slightly and marking the surface with a fine skewer; form a banana by rolling into a short log and curving it. Place the shaped fruits on baking paper and leave to dry for 1 to 2 hours. Once the surface has dried slightly, use a fine brush and gel food colours to paint the fruits: dilute the gel colour with a tiny drop of water and apply with light strokes. Build up colour gradually. Leave to dry completely before boxing.
